item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heck man I really like this SCP, but I think the headcanon I built up while reading it may not have been intended, going by the comments? But hey, the writing still lit my brain up like a Christmas tree, so I appreciate it nonetheless!

Basically the strong vibe I was getting from this object was that it's another culture's, era's, or universe's SCP-2000. It's a big, crazy, reality-warping compound, with containment chambers and even staff, and it ultimately seeks to hit the reset button on our world. Heck, I thought leaning on 2000 so hard in the containment procedures was clever foreshadowing!

But yeah I've had a blast reading and thinking about this, so major kudos!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t want to get into some big discussion about the writing styles or whatever, but the fact that it’s such a long, detailed article complete with exploration logs, interview logs, multiple pictures, and interactive elements (stuff you can click on, I guess) makes it one of my favorite articles. A lot of skips seem to have short or undetailed documents, and while some can turn out really great that way, I very much enjoy ones like this that take time to read and give you something to think about. If I could do it, I would give +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Mess" might be overstating it, but the length is indeed an issue - I found myself losing any sense of the attempt at the emotional impact in the mire of "I want to get through this already." Long SCPs CAN be emotionally impactful, but they have to carry and build it well, and this mostly just kind of coasted off of an early exploration log.

One other thing to think about in a rewrite: "confused" is an emotion that interferes with other emotions. This is clearly going for some mysterious elements, but the effective unsolved mystery answers enough of the little questions along the way that it feeds into the big unanswered ones at the end. I think this leaves too many unknowns in the process that it loses the reader and they can't think about the big picture implications. Speaking as someone who gets SUPER creeped out by the big unknown, you could hit hard with this one if you sent it down the right path, so I'd be interested in seeing how it turns out.

(Also, this is more personal preference so you can feel free to ignore it, but I feel like the the general tone is excessively fatalistic. It seems to me that the Foundation would try to do something to prevent the worst-case scenario rather than merely concede to it, impediments or neutralization proposals of some sort. The fallback option is good to have if they can't stop it, but just letting it be without doing a thing to get in the way of a K-class scenario seems not in the spirit of the Foundation to me. But again, you need not put any more weight on it than you feel like.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author here. It's been about a year and I think this article is a long-winded mess. Seconding most of the complaints regarding the length and firmly self-downvoting until I can rewrite this to do the concept more justice sometime in the next few months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get how they reached those conclusions, especially the exact timeline, it seems like the eventuality of it relesing a bunch of toxins and invasive speices is something that they have enough evidence for to justify preparing for but I would exspect them to, I dunno, try to actualy learn something about it, for that matter the divers seem like amitures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be "that guy" but I've gotta link you to a thread I just posted: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-4558848/a-desperate-plea-from-an-air-traffic-controller#post-3657538

everyone's use of "affirmative" makes me cringe when I read it. please take it to heart and share it around

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi,I am a Chinese translator, and I am trying to translate SCP-3069 to Chinese to help more people enjoy it, but I cannot understand some words in your article. Can you tell me what does " Innova-Exonera"mean?
In addition, that is really a wonderful article, and my most favourite type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just saying, I think it should be "[DATA EXPUNGED PER O5 ORDER]" not "[REMOVED ON ORDER OF O5 COUNCIL]"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this article. Great exploration log and dialogue tone, impressive scale, handled well in your writing, and satisfying questions and answers. I agree with some criticisms that it starts off with a large enough concept that the heightened dread is almost impossible to maintain through the rest of the piece, but I feel the rest of it was written well enough to get my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Third image in the article still says SCP-3000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really enjoyed reading that first exploration log. It was early morning here and it definitely made the hair on the back of my neck stand up.

The deep ocean has a unique type of horror, and the imagery that you used was effective.

For my own reading, I felt that the article was lacking an emotional climax. Working from the point of interview log 2A onwards, the article become progressively less engaging and more impersonal. Although the points about the creatures themselves are certainly necessary, I might suggest the addition of a moment where the curtains are thrown back and a more definitive reason for the O5's rationale is given. Where did this thing come from? Why are the inhabitants interested in people? How long have they been doing this?

As a personal aside I might recommend shrinking the size of the construct itself. I think that horror often incorporates an element of hope, and a construct the size of a small continent bent on exterminating humanity does little to engender hope. More intellectually, I think that our emotional responses are only really effective at the scale of the individual- meaning that although I'd be disturbed to learn of this thing IRL, I wouldn't necessarily be scared of it. The inverse is true for something like 106,which I find terrifying despite the relatively low overall threat level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are parts of this that are incredible that other people have touched on: exploration log 1A, interview log 2A, the drone exploration, and the containment procedures (their sheer scale set me up for "this thing is gonna be terrifying") really stood out to me.

But I felt like a lot of the rest of the article was long and unnecessary, primarily the translations and some of the "they went out and found some weird animals" bits. I feel like the latter logs could be condensed in some way. Also, it didn't really end to my taste. I was expecting it to go a little further or end with an "oh shit" moment, but it didn't. It ended almost clinically, just kinda summarizing what the reader already could glean from the article. I felt let down from all that buildup.

+1 because the parts of this that are good are really good, and I can see the rest reaching their level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One compliment I can give 3069 is that I wasn't bored once. The various documents and formats and clearance levels feel justified by the sheer physical size of the Object. Also, Exploration Log 1A felt very good. (Except that I was frustrated that they didn't just reel the team in when it became obvious that they had been mentally compromised. When I read the line (Agent Jones did not) allow the vehicle crew to reel in the safety line for a faster exit, my immediate reaction was an incredulous "Oh yeah? How'd he stop them?" Other than that, it was a great log.

Unfortunately, the author has a bad habit of burying the lede. Was this rushed or something to make it in under the contest deadline? Because it kind of feels more and more like a draft nearer to the end. There were moments where a character would nonchalantly refer to a fact when talking to another character, and I'd go "wait when did we establish that? Did I skip a line?" And I'd drop everything and scroll up, maybe use my web browser's search function, only to eventually get back to my current point in the story and realize that, nope, I didn't miss something, the relevant information just wasn't there.

Highlights include me muttering things like "Wait, what are the extradimensional capabilities as SCP-3069-A? And why wasn't that in the Description? I thought it was just some kinda cloaking device for Access Point III." "How do they know the person who looked into the camera done was a foundation employee?" and "Wait, what do they mean the person who looked into the camera 'resembles one of the missing personnel?' It's either the same person or else it isn't. If it is, why don't they just call them by name? If it isn't, why do they describe it as a foundation employee, and not "a person/entity wearing what appears to be a foundation uniform?" "Wait, what temporal anomalies!?" Leaps like this made me feel like the authors of the document (both in-universe and its actual author) know more than they're saying but aren't presenting the relevant information, and half the time when they do, it isn't in a digestible order.

(Remember, in-universe, these documents are meant to be instructive. They were deliberately written by their authors so that anyone not already in the loop could be brought up to speed just by reading them.)

Then the ending comes, and it just kinda sits there. An entire paragraph from an O5 basically saying "It's the end of the world. But we knew that already." And since we were all reminded of the plan to press the SCP-2000 Reset Button (at, frankly, the appropriate place in the story, the Containment Procedures,) any drama that might come from hearing the extent and inevitability of the XK is lost.

The first two complaints can be fixed with a little editing. Explicitly mention important details in the Descriptions before having characters refer back to them. I'm not really sure how to improve the ending. I was expecting… I don't know. Some sort of big reveal, even if it was so high up the Clearance food chain that it was just one O5 texting another O5, about the purpose or nature of this thing. Or some philosophical point that casts the whole skip in a new light. Or something. You know? It's possible the whole time travel thing was supposed to have weighty implications that I didn't pick up on. If so, that needs to be made a little more explicit.

All things considered, an ambitious attempt. I look forward to reading more from Cyantreuse

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Over" means that you are done sending and you are now receiving. If you keep talking you are just going to step on other transmissions. You should never have radio dialog where a transmission continues after "over".

I changed these just now (thank you), but the rest of the stuff you and Scorp are talking about would be too much of an overhaul for something that I really see no need to fix in terms of votes. If this were lower-rated, I would feel like putting effort into fixing the length and contextual errors like what you're pointing out and some stuff Moose pointed out, but as it stands it's probably going to hit +100 a few days after the contest settles down anyway, and when the only goals I really had were to not be in the last few places of the contest rankings and to get the number in Series IV I wanted, I honestly don't see a need to put any more writing effort into this clusterfuck of an article when I have other stuff on my list. Just different personal standards in what is or is not a "successful article", I guess, and I know I probably have lower standards in this light than most people with my number of posted articles. Just a personal prioritization difference I guess. Either way, thank you for pointing out the 'over' usage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, based on the creepy detail, the realistic (and often compelling) dialogue, and the well-thought-out premise, but it doesn't pay off enough for me to upvote, yet.

It really does need to be cut way down; nearly everything here is very well-written, but much of it is unnecessary — not adding much to the SCP. You only need some of the technical fluff to make it believable as a scientific document; most of the detail ought. (True realism won't be achieved either way; a realistic version of this document would be many, many times longer.)

I'd like to see more exploration of the concept — the mystery intelligence, the creatures, etc. The puppeteering is super creepy, but the rest isn't memorable. This also reminds me a little too strongly of books in the vein of "Sphere" — and I've seen the terraformer/alien 'ark' thing quite a lot on this site over the years. The fact that I've seen this concept before doesn't make it a bad one — but it does mean I want to see more interesting details that make it feel fresh and new.

Have some nitpicks
– hide block

There's no reason to have Site Director approval necessary in addition to two O5s. O5s are generally understood to be able to get their way no matter what anyway — headcanons may differ, but even so, that just serves as space filler.

There are quite a few very minor details like that throughout, either 'this science is slightly off', or "it's hard to believe the Foundation works this way".

An example of each:

  • Using the phrase "accelerated artificial evolutionary advancement". Technically, there's nothing wrong with this, but it evokes inaccurate but popular perceptions of evolution being 'advancing', as opposed to 'adapting'. It seems like it's trying to shorthand reference a soft sci-fi trope; odd for a document with so much scientific detail.
  • Removing some physical description and only letting a few O5s access it. I can't imagine any reason why this would be the case, and it comes off silly, since I'm already suspending my disbelief for an old-style expunge-gruesome-details redaction. Hurts suspension of disbelief.
– hide block

(Also, I really love the title.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hope this wins

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, but they are very short range and aren't going to work through the walls of this structure besides. I'm not aware of any radio technology that would allow the submarine to communicate with the divers as described in the logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Water's not totally opaque to radio waves…more like "frosted". They make scuba gear with walkie-talkie type radios built into them, for example: they have to be way stronger than ones for land and don't have huge amounts of range, but they exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this has moments where the tension and creepiness come through, but overall this is significantly overdeveloped and largely squanders any impact it might have had by the end. A global ecological collapse in 250 years is a fairly abstract threat and while this does attempt to drive it home in a more personal way, it doesn't really work.

There's a huge technical problem too: water is opaque to radio wavelengths, this is why submarines aren't able to communicate without surfacing (yes ELF receivers work close to the surface, but these both require very long antennas — long enough that the cable probably could just connect to the sub without additional length — and have such low bandwidth that real-time voice simply isn't possible, it takes several minutes to receive even a few lines of text — think telegraphy).

Also:

That is ill-advised, over. There is no telling what a transportation device of unknown function will do to human physiology. If you decide to proceed, do not go alone, and keep in mind that it could have adverse effects on your body.

"Over" means that you are done sending and you are now receiving. If you keep talking you are just going to step on other transmissions. You should never have radio dialog where a transmission continues after "over".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My assumption would be rising sea levels due to glaciers and whatnot melting. Releasing a lot of sulfur and carbons into the atmosphere would lead to greenhousing on an accelerating timeline. The shoreline would quickly go from where it is, to the Appalachia in the US. France, Spain, and Portugal on the mainland of Europe would also see serious flooding, and the British Isles would likely submerge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article had really good buildup, but it just left me waiting for a climax that never happened. I really wanted to like this for a number of reasons. The exploration logs where great, the containment procedures gave a real sense of "Oh Shit!", but the ending was really unsatisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I found out what this thing is. It's SCP-001-DJ-TG. The Gulf of Mexico (Where SCP-001-DJ-TG was buried) is very close to the Atlantic geographically. Remember, it wasn't exclusively a CoBG anomaly, it just took advantage of the CoBG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, excuse me? There appears to be a mistake. I believe I ordered the LARGE SCP-3000?

Jesus Christ, could you have made this longer? (Not necessarily a bad thing, just worthy of pointing out)

Also, this article isn't exactly horrific. It didn't really leave me on edge like SCP-1730 or SCP-455, but rather left me curious. I think you spent too much time going into detail about this (again, just worthy of pointing out) rather than indulging in how this may be horrific. The only real thing that gave me chills was the entity in the exploration log, but other than that I'm just curious. And I don't really know what it is im curious about, so sorry about that.

You have something good here, I can feel it, but not really worth the title of SCP-3000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. There is certainly an admirable scope to this article, but getting through it is tedious, and the creepy parts are too few and far between for this to be an effective horror article for me, or even a creepy article for me.

I really like the exploration log, and the implications of Lockwood's return. But those get drowned out by so much noise that what creeped me out about those parts gets buried. You hit on so many different kinds of horror in this article, but never really settle on one to dive into and drive the point home:

  • Are we to be scarred of the creatures emerging from this thing? If so, we need to see more of them, and a lot more of the damage they can do.
  • Are we to be jarred by the intelligence behind this contraption? If that is the case, give us more insight into what makes that intelligence click.
  • Are we to be disturbed by the futility of fighting this thing? In that scenario, I'd like to see more of the Foundation's actions and how they go awry.

There is fertile ground down all these avenues, but right now you only give me a taste of each, and not enough of a taste to be satisfying.

Reluctant -1. There are parts of this article that are awesome-sauce, but it is so bloated and gets pulled in so many directions that it gets watered down into just sauce.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, the toxic gunk and creatures coming out of that area, elaborated upon in Site-42's test logs.

But how are the toxic sea creatures and toxic sea gunk going to affect humans that are miles from the coast?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I usually find myself skimming the containment procedures of an SCP object, then rereading them after I've fully understood the description. Yours, however, gripped me from the start, and I didn't even touch the description until I'd fully pored over all the collapsibles. Way to build a looming threat!

That being said, I feel like the rest of the article winds up meandering and distracting from the initial terror. This SCP is defined by scale, and for the sheer amount of detail provided here, I ended up not knowing nearly as much as I wanted to. I feel like there's a canon or a series of tales just ready to come from this thing. Appropriate to its size, this SCP strikes me as just too gargantuan for one page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a bad idea, and I find it well written and intresting. However, it breaches on territory already covered, what with the "aliens/extradimensional beings/ superpowers releasing monsters from below the ocean" that pacific rim and SCP-2406 already covered, and the "giant underwater object that releases monsters" that SCP-169 is known for. Tentative upvote for now though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the path taken here of focusing on the effects of the anomaly rather than on its intentions. Explanation isn't a must, and I suspect that doing so might not add much, and could possibly detract from the unsettling nature of the SCP. I feel that the thrust of the article is in the Foundation's attitude to the implications of an end-of-the-world scenario; seeing the use of SCP-2000 as part of the containment procedures in such an as-per-protocol manner is a very strong opener. As other reviewers have expressed, however, there isn't ever really a moment of dreaded revelation or anything that truly stands out as climactic. This feels like a slow-burn horror piece that ends in a quiet, somewhat contemplative fizzle.

There are certainly some memorable bits, in particular the encounter with the entity in the first exploration log (which as a whole is exemplary). There are similarly vivid encounters elsewhere in the piece, but it feels unsatisfying that each individual aspect of the anomaly (the entities, the light, and interior itself) gets so little screen-time. It's almost agonising, because what little is explicitly revealed is golden.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rest of this post makes total sense but is too large for me to 'fix', so to speak, at this time, though I may come back to it in a few days since editing is allowed until the end of the posting period. (And props to you for thinking of the cable thing, because I completely did not consider that.) This part though:

4) The article doesn't seem to specify why evacuation of Atlantic coastal areas would be needed, or why it would eventually lead to human extinction. Sulfurous fumes released by the ocean making the air unbreathable?

No, the toxic gunk and creatures coming out of that area, elaborated upon in Site-42's test logs. That's why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Show critiques
-hide critiques

1)

Agent Jones was equipped with standard two-way radio equipment for the purposes of communicating with observation personnel. All three agents were equipped with full-body locking restraint harnesses attached to a cord rappelling system, …

Due to transmissions complications caused by the underwater depth of SCP-3000 and the unknown composition of SCP-3000's exterior, real-time video transmission could not be established.

Since they already have cables attached, rather than using radio it'd make more sense for the cables to include wires or fiber-optics for both voice communication and to stream back the video feeds. I can understand why you'd want no real-time video for narrative reasons, but this just feels off.

2) Any scientific analysis of the unknown lifeforms would include analyzing their DNA to see the differences and similarities to known species.

3) The article mentions "forced/accelerated evolution", but that would imply changing the DNA of species already present on Earth. What seems to be happening is the replacement of existing species with new species.

4) The article doesn't seem to specify why evacuation of Atlantic coastal areas would be needed, or why it would eventually lead to human extinction. Sulfurous fumes released by the ocean making the air unbreathable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not much I can add that hasn't been said. The first exploration log is the heart of the creep: I've never seen a log like that, that had to be narrated real-time before (as opposed to described via text or related afterward), and it went a long way toward selling the claustrophobia and bathophobia that this piece accomplishes well.

But I think "worldwide biological shift" is the real point here, but it's just such a huge thing, as kaktus said, it's hard to grasp on to for an "oh shit" moment. (Also, I'm in the low risk area, hooray!) And as others have said, it doesn't seem to build to anything much by the end. It's still a good piece, but I'll upvote after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, no worries. This is post-posting, of course, so it'd just be extra help anyway, and of course not obligatory. I do appreciate the pointers you gave in the post, to begin with. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let me see what I can do. RL is starting to come back in a big way (which has kept me from reviewing 3000 entry forum drafts too), but I'll let you know if I manage to get on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect that a few more big revisions to this could produce something very strong.

If you're present in chat sometime soon and have the time, I'd definitely like to hear any suggestions you have, given that updates are allowed until the end of the posting period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding some other people: There's a lot of good ideas here, but they don't really manage to come together in a way that has a huge impact.

I like the detail put into it (as a bio nerd, the detailing of the diagnostic tests used on the poor agent.) And I feel the strongest part is the very through containment procedures detailing the long term control and recovery from these events. The object itself and how people react to it are interesting, but what it lacks are maybe tonal elements that would actually make this anomaly feel viscerally threatening and scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is meaty, both in terms of the scientific detail, and the substance of the logs (for which I can appreciate the focus on safety and process, despite the difference in tone they create). There are also some good creepy details - the first half of the exploration, the description of Lockwood's return and the D-Class drowning, in particular.

For me, the article as a whole didn't do enough to drive home these strengths. Part of that comes from the fact that the Containment Procedures give away the ending (the world is toast), reducing the tension of "will the Foundation scrape through", but the rest of the document doesn't emphasise the horror of failure / inevitable doom to make up for it. Partly as well I think this ends with some of the weaker material - we go from a very unsettling exploration, and the great description of the "strings of color", to a relatively unsurprising translation, some short exploration vignettes that don't add much to the story other than complicating the anomaly further, a few lesions and heart attacks, and ultimately a confirmation of the explanation that could be intuited from the outset. That summary sounds facetious (as summaries can sometimes sound, and for which I apologise) and the individual parts are still well-written, but they felt less like propulsive additions to the overall narrative than additional loose ends.

I suspect that a few more big revisions to this could produce something very strong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I reviewed this, I said something about the disconcerting nature of the unknown. That unsettling feeling when you see preparations being made, but aren't given an explanation as to why.

The setup to this is astoundingly large in scale. Evacuating the entire eastern seaboard, activating SCP-2000, preparing for a 250 year long rebuilding process, but then very little in the way of why. The logs are vague, the explanations are vague, everything leads towards something nasty without outright saying it. And that's spooky.

There's enough here to leave me unsettled. I don't think this is perfect, conceptually, but it does more than enough to give me pause. I'll be thinking about it for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just never quite coalesces for me.

+ show block
– hide block

The O5s are dead certain of this thing's ultimate intentions, or at least impact. That isn't necessarily a bad thing.
It pumps out toxic creatures that seriously harm humans, and there are so many of them that it will force evolution. Not necessarily a bad thing.
It does strange things to humans. Definitely not a bad thing, although the body horror was way less interesting than them just being there. The best moments on that front were the corrupted D-Class drowning and the Foundation employee on the beach and air bubble, except before I read the specifics in the footnote. The reappearance of Lockwood seemed to ultimately just exist to provide atmosphere, ang it dragged a lot for that.
All of the characters represented in dialogue or writing seemed super concerned with protocol and safety and having solid evidence. Not necessarily a bad thing, a bold choice in fact, but it led to a lot of drag.
Weird incorporeal being fixing stuff? Quite cool, in fact, although the memetic/possession thing got muddled.
Ancient hieroglyphs that sound vaguely like containment procedures? Not new, but definitely not necessarily a bad thing.

But where does it all go? It just reads like this post-mortem, this documentation of a giant manhunt that consistently finds nothing conclusive except for one big dread answer. (I honestly don't know what they expect -2000 to accomplish if the whole biosphere changes.)

There is some horror in futility, and the tragic flaws built into an organisation by their structure and ethos, but the actions here read with their own unique tone compared to other SCPs, and not knowing at all just wasn't satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally understand, no worries. Particularly this:

I can't tell you what to cut or what not to cut, because I honestly am not knowledgeable enough about the topics at hand to pinpoint exactly what's superfluous or what's not.

I'm… not a marine biologist or anything similar. I knew the basics of how tectonic plates and sea depths work just because hobbies and memories from high school, but that's really it. I researched a lot for this, but that also means that I am not knowledgeable enough about the topics at hand to pinpoint exactly what's superfluous or what's not. So I can get this viewpoint for sure.

item.title: Nitpickery
item.content:encoded:
what a way to start off on the site lol
Putting in a specific year, particularly one in the future, strikes me as a bit odd…and definitely breaks my suspension of disbelief. A concept I've seen preached many times before is that of an "ambiguous time-slice"—emphasis on the "ambiguous". It makes it much easier to believe, as little of that as a piece of speculative fiction might expect (Aren't the second parts of years usually blocked out? Maybe try that; narrowing it down to a single century should be enough to get your point across). Other than that, this is a great article, and has my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's, I think, potential here for this to be engaging and horrifying at once. It does neither for me; I know what you're going for, but this felt like such a slog to read that I couldn't get anything out of it. Because of that, the horror was diluted for me majorly. I can't tell you what to cut or what not to cut, because I honestly am not knowledgeable enough about the topics at hand to pinpoint exactly what's superfluous or what's not.

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded:
Post-contest edit: 3000 entry is now 3069, because I'm mature like that. Cursed number. Good game, everyone.

Image sourcing:

  • map of North Atlantic; CC-by-SA-3.0 Wikimedia Commons
  • NOAA Image ID: expl7016; CC Basic 2.0
  • NOAA Image ID: map00257; CC Basic 2.0
  • Dredge pipe; public domain Wikimedia Commons
  • High-Pressure Gas Pipe; CC 2.0 Generic geograph.org.uk
  • Map of USA; CC-by-SA-3.0 Wikimedia Commons

Thank you to the following scientific and informational services for assisting me with the knowledge required to write this:

  • Wikipedia: Transatlantic telecommunications cable
  • Wikipedia: TAT-14
  • Wikipedia: DSVs
  • TAT-14 Cable System website
  • ArcGIS Ocean Basemap
  • Google Maps/Google Earth
  • Magnetic Units Converter
  • C/kg to Sv Converter
  • water depth to pressure converter
  • postmortem serotonin levels research

Thank you to the following reviewers:

Palmetto28, djkaktus, WrongJohnSilver, salvagebar, and my mother. Thanks, y'all; you're the best.
